items.q: Did Jesus use parables just to make his teaching simple for everyone?
items.correct: Not only - parables provoked thought and demanded a response; Mark 4 even suggests they concealed meaning from hostile hearers while revealing it to disciples.
items.misconception: Parables were merely simple illustrations everyone immediately understood.
items.random_wrong: Parables were songs sung in the synagogue.
items.why: Treating parables as easy fables misses the challenge they posed, which exam questions on their purpose target.

items.q: Did Jesus teach that the Jewish Law should be abandoned?
items.correct: No - in Matthew 5:17 he says he came 'not to abolish the Law but to fulfil it', and his teaching often deepens its demands rather than discarding them.
items.misconception: Jesus rejected the Law of Moses and told followers to ignore it.
items.random_wrong: Jesus wrote a new law code to replace Roman law.
items.why: The fulfil-not-abolish saying is set-text material; students who assume rejection misread the Sermon on the Mount.

items.q: Does 'turn the other cheek' mean Jesus approved of injustice?
items.correct: No - the teaching rejects personal revenge and calls for a creative, non-violent response; Jesus himself openly challenged wrongdoing.
items.misconception: Turning the other cheek means letting evil win and saying nothing.
items.random_wrong: The saying refers to turning away from people who are rude.
items.why: Students confuse renouncing retaliation with passivity; the distinction matters for questions on Jesus's ethics.

items.q: Which question did the parable of the good Samaritan answer?
items.why: Correct answer: 'Who is my neighbour?'. “'How many times should I forgive?'” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: With which teachings does the Sermon on the Mount open?
items.why: Correct answer: The Beatitudes. “The Ten Commandments” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: According to Jesus, what is the greatest commandment?
items.correct: To love God with all your heart, soul, mind and strength
items.why: It's To love God with all your heart, soul, mind and strength. “To keep the Sabbath” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Jesus's saying 'seventy-seven times' answers a question about what?
items.correct: How many times to forgive someone
items.misconception: How many loaves fed the crowd
items.random_wrong: How long to pray each day
items.why: It's How many times to forgive someone. “How many loaves fed the crowd” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
